coredata.dc:title: Surface modification of carbon dots by boron and phosphorus to construct flame-retardant epoxy resin with high mechanical and low dielectric properties
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lingzhi
Liu, Hua
Zeng, Birong
He, Kaibin
Xu, Yiting
Yuan, Conghui
Dai, Lizong
coredata.dc:description: The effective combination of flame retardant element and carbon dots for the development of epoxy composites with high mechanical property and low dielectric constant in the field of flame retardant material is still a challenge. Herein, the boron and phosphorus dual-element doped carbon dots (BP&NCDs) was synthesized using a simple Kabachnik-Fields reaction, and then incorporated into epoxy resin in different amount to prepare a series of EP/BP&NCDs composites. The optical, thermal stability, mechanical, dielectric and flame-retardant properties were tested by many techniques such as UV–vis spectroscopy, FL, TEM, TG, UL-94 and LOI e.g. It showed that the EP/BP&NCDs composites had favorable transparency, hydrophobicity, mechanical and dielectric properties, while maintaining the photo-luminescent properties. It was worth noting that the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite with only 3 % BP&NCDs addition exhibited significant flame-retardant properties with a UL-94 V-0 rating and a limiting oxygen index (LOI) of 31.3 %. Meanwhile, it led to obvious reductions of 34.9 %, 26.6 %, and 15.6 % in peak heat release rate (PHRR), total heat release (THR), and total smoke production (TSP), respectively. Furthermore, compared with pure EP, the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites enhanced the bending strength and impact strength by 32.3 % and 30.3 %, but reduced the dielectric constant and dielectric loss by about 40.0 % and 50.0 %, respectively. When EP/BP&NCDs composites were burned, a continuous and dense carbon layer was formed due to the synergistic action of phosphorus and boron in CDs. Overall the carbon dots (CDs) after surface modification could be applied in epoxy resin system for the achievement of multiple functional EP composites with good flame retardancy, mechanical and dielectric property at low addition, which is helpful to provide a universal strategy in this field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Flame retardant
Epoxy composites
Carbon dot
Surface modification
Hybrid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure and optical properties of BP&NCDs
Thermal mechanical properties of EP/BP&NCDs composites
Mechanical properties and static water contact angle of EP/BP&NCDs composites
Flame retardant performance of EP/BP&NCDs composites
The characterization of residual carbon in EP/BP&NCDs composites
Flame retardant mechanism of EP/BP&NCDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation route of the hybrid carbon dot BP&NCDs.
FTIR spectra of BP&NCDs (a), XRD pattern (b), TEM image (c), particle size distribution plot (d), and TEM mapping images (e).

(a) The UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of BP&NCDs, (b) the fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelengths (c = 1500 ppm), and (c) the fluorescence spectra at different concentrations (λex = 385 nm).

(a) The EP/BP&NCDs samples under natural light and 365 nm ultraviolet light, (b) ultraviolet transmittance spectra (b), fluorescence spectra (c), and fluorescence spectra of EP/BP&NCDs composites under different emission wavelengths (d).
The TG and DTG curves of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites under N2 atmosphere (a-b) and air atmosphere(c-d).
The E′(a), tan δ (b) and DSC (c) of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites.

EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites' three-point bending images (a), stress-strain curves (b), flexural strength and modulus (c), impact strength (d), fracture SEM images (e), etched SEM images (f), and contact angle (g).
Dielectric constant (a) and dielectric loss (b) of EP/BP&NCDs composites.
UL-94 digital images (a) and LOI values (b) and HRR (c) and THR (d) and TSP (e) of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites.
The cone calorimetry residue digital image (a) of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites, SEM images of the exterior (b) and interior (c) of the cone calorimetry residue, and Raman spectroscopy (d).

TG-FTIR image of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites (a) and IR spectra of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites at different temperatures during decomposition (b) and flame retardant mechanism diagram of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites (c).
DMA and DSC data for EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites.
Cone calorimetric data of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites.

head.ce:title: Surface modification of carbon dots by boron and phosphorus to construct flame-retardant epoxy resin with high mechanical and low dielectric properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aFujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bXiamen Key Laboratory of Fire Retardant Materials, College of Materials, Xiamen University, Xiamen, 361005, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effective combination of flame retardant element and carbon dots for the development of epoxy composites with high mechanical property and low dielectric constant in the field of flame retardant material is still a challenge. Herein, the boron and phosphorus dual-element doped carbon dots (BP&NCDs) was synthesized using a simple Kabachnik-Fields reaction, and then incorporated into epoxy resin in different amount to prepare a series of EP/BP&NCDs composites. The optical, thermal stability, mechanical, dielectric and flame-retardant properties were tested by many techniques such as UV–vis spectroscopy, FL, TEM, TG, UL-94 and LOI e.g. It showed that the EP/BP&NCDs composites had favorable transparency, hydrophobicity, mechanical and dielectric properties, while maintaining the photo-luminescent properties. It was worth noting that the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite with only 3 % BP&NCDs addition exhibited significant flame-retardant properties with a UL-94 V-0 rating and a limiting oxygen index (LOI) of 31.3 %. Meanwhile, it led to obvious reductions of 34.9 %, 26.6 %, and 15.6 % in peak heat release rate (PHRR), total heat release (THR), and total smoke production (TSP), respectively. Furthermore, compared with pure EP, the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites enhanced the bending strength and impact strength by 32.3 % and 30.3 %, but reduced the dielectric constant and dielectric loss by about 40.0 % and 50.0 %, respectively. When EP/BP&NCDs composites were burned, a continuous and dense carbon layer was formed due to the synergistic action of phosphorus and boron in CDs. Overall the carbon dots (CDs) after surface modification could be applied in epoxy resin system for the achievement of multiple functional EP composites with good flame retardancy, mechanical and dielectric property at low addition, which is helpful to provide a universal strategy in this field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Epoxy resin (EP) is a commonly employed thermosetting resin, capable of establishing a robust three-dimensional network structure upon curing. Renowned for its superior mechanical properties, bonding performance, electrical insulation capabilities, and processability, EP is widely applied across various industrial and civil sectors, including aerospace, automobile manufacturing, construction, electronics, shipbuilding, coatings, and adhesives [1–5]. However, EP possesses a large number of CC and CO bonds which result in its flammable nature, and EP exhibits poor toughness and impact resistance, and these drawbacks severely limit the application of EP in high-end fields [6–9]. In order to adapt to the development of the times and enable EP to be applied in the artificial intelligence and electronic information industries, modern technology has imposed higher requirements on EP in terms of flame retardancy, transparency, mechanical properties, and special functionalities [10–13].

Adding flame retardants to EP was an important and feasible solution at the time to endow it with excellent flame retardant properties. Halogen-based flame retardants were once popular due to their excellent flame retardant performance. However, they were criticized for their environmental and human health risks, leading to their gradual withdrawal from the historical stage [14]. Currently, halogen-free phosphorus-based traditional flame retardants were widely welcomed because they could endow polymers with excellent flame retardant properties while being environmentally and human-friendly. Phosphorus-containing flame retardants not only capture a large number of phosphorus-free radicals and reactive free radicals generated during combustion but also promote the formation of char, thereby enhancing the flame retardancy of polymers. But still, the addition of phosphorus-containing flame retardants often caused negative impacts on the transparency, mechanical properties, and other aspects of EP. Therefore, it is extremely important for EP applications to achieve excellent flame retardant performance while maintaining its overall performance [15–19].

In recent years, due to their superior performance, carbon-based materials have been widely used in the production of polymer materials, providing new avenues to strengthen the flame-retardant properties of polymer materials while maintaining overall performance. Following carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerenes, one novel kind of carbon nanomaterial that had surfaced was carbon dots (CDs), due to their photoluminescent properties (PL), low toxicity, rich functional groups, and chemical stability [20–23]. On one hand, CDs exhibited tremendous potential in the field of LED devices due to their excellent optical properties and high stability [24–26]. For example, the preparation of white light carbon dots that spanned the entire visible spectrum could provide new insights for white light LED technology [27]. The active emission layer composed of pure blue carbon dots can be used to prepare pure blue LED materials [28], among others. On the other hand, similar to traditional carbon-based materials, CDs made significant contributions to enhancing the flame-retardant properties of polymers [29]. Liu et al. [30] synthesized fluorescent carbon dots and integrated them into polystyrene (PS) to enhance flame resistance. Bifulco et al. [31] synthesized carbon dots to fabricate fire-resistant, hydrophobic, and flame-triggered shape memory epoxy nanocomposites. The exceptional optical and flame-retardant properties exhibited by CDs align closely with the demands placed on EP in the electronic information sector during the information age. However, enhancing the flame-retardant performance of materials with CDs often involved increasing the loading ratio, which could significantly impact the transparency and mechanical properties of the materials. Therefore, further research was warranted to explore how to integrate CDs with EP in a manner that delivers outstanding performance.

Fortunately, the abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs provide significant opportunities for their molecular modification in polymers [32,33]. Lu et al. [34] employed 4-(bromomethyl)phenylboronic acid to modify amino-rich carbon dots, thereby effectively enabling their utilization in intracellular protein delivery. Zheng et al. [35] modified carbon dots with vitamin C and formaldehyde to serve as nano-probes in bioimaging applications. Therefore, we proposed a method of surface modification of CDs to reduce their loading ratio in the material while enhancing flame retardancy without compromising other properties. This provided a new approach for the application of CDs in the epoxy resin field.

In this study, 4,4′-diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM) was chosen as a carbon and nitrogen resource to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs), because DDM was the curing agent of diglycidyl ether bisphenol A (DGEBA) and could dissolve well in epoxy resin. The surface modification of NCDs by reacting with 4-formylphenylboronic acid (FPBA) and 9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide (DOPO) was further conducted to obtain green-fluorescent BP&NCDs, which affected the optical, thermal stability, mechanical, dielectric and flame-retardant properties of the epoxy composites EP/BP&NCDs by varying the addition at 1 wt%, 3 wt%, and 5 wt%. Various testing results showed that epoxy composites maintained high transparency and exhibited photoluminescence capability, while also enhancing flame retardancy, toughness, and hydrophobic properties with lower dielectric constant and dielectric loss. Additionally, the flame-retardant mechanism of the epoxy composites was explored. This research offered a feasible approach for developing flame-retardant epoxy resins in the field of electronic industry.

4,4′-Diaminodiphenylmethane (DDM, 97 %), 4-formylphenylboronic acid (FPBA, 97 %), and 9,10-dihydro-9-oxa-10-phosphaphenanthrene-10-oxide (DOPO, 97 %) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethanol was offered by Shantou Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. DGEBA (commercial brand: E-51, epoxy value is 0.51 mol/100 g) was offered by Longyan Resin Factory.

NCDs were prepared via a solvent-thermal method [36]. Firstly, DDM (1g) was completely dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (15 mL) under magnetic stirring. The final mixture was poured into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless-steel high-pressure reaction vessel with a 25 mL capacity. Then, the vessel was heated at 180 °C for 14 h in a blast drying oven. After the reaction vessel temperature dropped to room temperature, the solution was taken out and spun for 20 mins at 11,000 rpm. Subsequently, a 0.22 µm ultrafiltration membrane was used to filter the centrifuged solution in order to eliminate impurities. Finally, the filtered solution was vacuum rotary evaporated to obtain solid NCDs with a yellow-brown color. Finally, 0.827 g of NCDs was obtained, and the yield was 82.7 %.

BP&NCDs were synthesized via the Kabachnik-Fields reaction. NCDs, FPBA, and DOPO were used in a molar ratio of 1:2:2 for the synthesis of BP&NCDs. NCDs (0.02 mol, 3.964 g) and FPBA (0.04 mol, 5.996 g) were added to a 100 mL three-necked flask with a condenser, followed that 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added to dissolve them. Under a nitrogen atmosphere, the reaction was run for 4 h at 60 °C. The reaction was then extended for an additional 14 h by adding a solution of DOPO (0.04 mol, 8.644 g) dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (25 mL) to the reaction mixture. After the reaction, the solution was vacuum rotary evaporated. When the concentrated solution reached about 20 mL, it was slowly added to ultrapure water to precipitate the product. After three repeated precipitations, vacuum filtration was performed under reduced pressure. Finally, the precipitate dried for 24 h in a vacuum oven to obtain white powder BP&NCDs, with a yield of approximately 81 %. The preparation process was illustrated in Fig. 1 .

As shown in Fig. 5(a), the preparation of EP samples and EP/BP&NCDs composites was carried out using the DGEBA/DDM system. Taking EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material as an example, the specific process for preparation was as follows: 10 g of DGEBA were mixed with 0.375 g of BP&NCDs that had been weighed and dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol. The mixture was thoroughly mixed at 70 °C and vacuumed in a double-tube vacuum pump for 3 h to remove ethanol and bubbles. Subsequently, weighing out 2.5 g of DDM powder, we added it to the mixture and stirred it at 95 °C until the DDM dissolved completely. In the end, the epoxy mixture was put into an aluminum mold that had been heated to 80 °C in a blast-drying oven for 30 min. It was then allowed to cure for 2 h at 120 °C, 4 h at 140 °C, and 2 h at 180 °C in the oven. EP, EP/1 %-BP&NCDs, and EP/5 %-BP&NCDs were also prepared utilizing the same methodology.
The details of the instruments could be found in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of BP&NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The raw materials NCDs, DOPO, FPBA, and the product BP&NCDs were examined using FTIR spectroscopy, 1H NMR, 31P NMR and 11B NMR. The characteristic DOPO peaks could be seen in Fig. 2(a), where they corresponded to the stretching vibrations of P-O, P = O, and Ar-H, respectively, and appear at 928 cm−1, 1200 cm−1, and 1590 cm−1. Furthermore, the infrared spectrum of the product BP&NCDs did not show the characteristic peak for the P-H stretching vibration of DOPO at 2380 cm⁻¹, indicating that DOPO had completely reacted [37]. Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks of the raw material FPBA at 1340 cm−1 (B-O stretching vibration) and 3240 cm−1 (B-OH stretching vibration) appeared in BP&NCDs’ IR spectrum. Additionally, the characteristic peaks of FPBA at 2850 cm⁻¹ (O = CH stretching vibration) and 1670 cm⁻¹ (C = O stretching vibration) disappeared in the IR spectrum of BP&NCDs, illustrating the reaction of the C = O bond and confirming that FPBA was successfully introduced [38]. Moreover, in the IR spectrum of BP&NCDs, the characteristic NH and CN (aromatic carbon) stretching vibration peaks at 3430 and 3330 cm−1 and 1280 cm−1, respectively, vanished. Furthermore, a characteristic peak of CN (alkyl carbon) stretching vibration appeared at 1120 cm−1, indicating the reaction of the NH bond [39]. The successful synthesis of BP&NCDs was also verified by NMR characterization (Fig. S1). In the 1H NMR spectra, the peak at 8.73 ppm corresponded to the H atom on the P-H bond in DOPO (Fig. S1a), the peaks at 10.04 ppm and 3.46 ppm corresponded to the H atoms on -CHO and B-OH in FPBA, respectively (Fig. S1b), and the peak at 4.78 ppm corresponded to the H atom on -NH2 in NCDs (Fig. S1c). In the 1H NMR spectrum of BP&NCDs (Fig. S1d), the peak at 3.46 ppm corresponded to the H atom on B-OH, while the peaks at 3.33 ppm and 8.00 ppm corresponded to the newly formed -CH- and -NH- groups, without the appearance of the signals of P-H, -CHO, and -NH2. In the 31P NMR spectrum, the peaks at 14.27 ppm and 13.01 ppm corresponded to the phosphorus atoms in DOPO (Fig. S1e), while BP&NCDs exhibited a double peak phenomenon at 31.06 ppm and 28.36 ppm due to possible steric hindrance from the phosphine oxide group, confirming the reaction with DOPO (Fig. S1f) [3]. Additionally, in the 11B NMR spectrum (Fig. S1 g), BP&NCDs also showed signals attributed to the B atoms similar to FPBA [40], confirming the grafting with FPBA.

The XRD pattern of the product BP&NCDs was shown in Fig. 2(b). Apparently, a broad diffraction peak was observed around 2θ=21°, in accordance with the diffraction of the graphite carbon (002) plane, which was mainly due to the low crystallinity [31]. The TEM image and particle size distribution of BP&NCDs were displayed in Figs. 2(c) and (d), respectively. It could be seen that BP&NCDs were spherically and uniformly distributed with a diameter range of 1.0 ∼ 4.6 nm. The average diameter was calculated to be about 2.6 nm. As Fig. 2(e) illustrates, the elemental mapping revealed the elemental composition of BP&NCDs. It was found that the elements of C, O, N, P, and B were uniformly distributed on the surface of BP&NCDs.

To confirm the chemical structure of BP&NCDs, XPS characterization was conducted. The high-resolution spectrum of BP&NCDs included five peaks corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s, P 2p, and B 1 s, as shown in Fig. 3(a). The presence of phosphorus and boron elements in BP&NCDs indicated the successful introduction of DOPO and FPBA. Four fitted peaks at 283.4 eV, 284.5 eV, 285.1 eV, and 286.1 eV were found in the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of BP&NCDs, as indicated in Fig. 3(b), which were attributed to the C-B, C = C, CC/C-P, and CN/CO bonds, respectively. [41–45]. The OH, ON, and O-B bonds were responsible for the peaks in the O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) located at 531.4 eV, 532.8 eV, and 534.1 eV, respectively [41]. Fig. 3(d) illustrated NH and NC bonds were responsible for the peaks in the N 1 s XPS spectrum at 399.7 eV and 401.6 eV, respectively [43]. As shown in Fig. 3e, P-C, P = O, and P-O bonds were responsible for the peaks in the P 2p XPS spectrum at 132.9 eV, 133.6 eV, and 134.9 eV, respectively [42], which indicated that DOPO was successfully introduced during the synthesis process. B-C and B-O bonds were identified as the sources of the peaks in the B 1 s XPS spectrum at 188.8 eV and 191.2 eV, respectively [45], as shown in Fig. 3(f), which indicated that FPBA was successfully introduced during the synthesis process. In a word, the BP&NCDs hybrid had been successfully synthesized.

The UV–vis spectrum of BP&NCDs, as shown in Fig. 4(a), exhibited absorption peaks at 275 nm and 300 nm, corresponding to the n→π* and π→π* transitions of the C = C bond and the CN bond in the benzene ring, respectively. The prepared BP&NCDs were completely dissolved in ethanol, appearing pale yellow in sunlight and emitting green fluorescence under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm. According to Fig. 4(b) and (c), BP&NCDs exhibited fluorescence emission at 490 nm under different excitation wavelengths, and as the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence intensity first showed an increasing trend and then a decreasing trend, with the optimal excitation wavelength being 385 nm. Additionally, at different concentrations, as the excitation wavelength increased, the fluorescence intensity also showed a similar trend of increasing and then decreasing, with the highest fluorescence intensity observed at a concentration of 2000 ppm. This phenomenon might be related to the functional groups present on the surface of BP&NCDs.

As shown in Fig. 5(a), BP&NCDs exhibited good compatibility with EP, and the EP/BP&NCDs composite prepared by adding 5 % of BP&NCDs remained transparent under natural light. Furthermore, According to Fig. 5(b), in the UV–vis spectrum, the EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited good transmittance in the range of 200 - 800 nm, even EP/5 %-BP&NCDs could clearly see the maple leaf shape at the bottom. Additionally, based on the fluorescence characteristics exhibited by BP&NCDs themselves, we investigated the fluorescence properties of EP/BP&NCDs composites. Fig. 5(a) also showed that, under natural light, both EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites appeared in a pale yellow color. When subjected to 365 nm ultraviolet light, EP did not fluoresce, but under 365 nm ultraviolet excitation, EP/BP&NCDs composites emitted excitation light at 460 nm, exhibiting blue-green fluorescence, as depicted in Fig. 5(c). In Fig. 5(d), under different ultraviolet emission lights ranging from 365 to 435 nm, excitation light emitted by EP/BP&NCDs composites experienced a redshift. This may be due to the fact that the grafted FPBA and DOPO could alter the local environment on the surface of the CDs, affecting the transition energy between the excited state and the fluorescent state, thereby causing a change in the fluorescence emission wavelength.

Thermogravimetric analysis was used to examine the thermal decomposition behavior of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites in an N2 atmosphere. The DTG curves of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited a single peak, with decomposition temperatures ranging from 350 to 400 °C, indicating a one-step decomposition process, which was a typical manifestation of epoxy resin network decomposition. This suggested that the addition of BP&NCDs to epoxy resin did not alter its original thermal decomposition mode, as shown in Fig. 6a. Combining Fig. 6a and Table S1, it was observed that in contrast to EP, the T5 % of EP/BP&NCDs composites cured with 1 %, 3 %, and 5 % low addition levels of BP&NCDs gradually decreased, while Tmax remained almost unchanged. For instance, the T5 % of pure EP was 357.98 °C, and Tmax was 381.94 °C, while for EP/5 %-BP&NCDs, the T5 % was 326.87 °C, and Tmax was 378.14 °C. The phenomenon could be attributed to two main reasons. First, due to its relatively low thermal stability, the successful incorporation of DOPO leads to the cleavage of the P-O-C bond in the benzene ring structure, which primarily occurs during the heating process, resulting in an earlier degradation to carbon [46], the second reason was the introduction of FPBA, as the amount of BP&NCDs added increased, the content of unreacted B-OH bonds, which did not participate in the curing of epoxy resin, also increased accordingly. During the heating decomposition process, water and boroxine were generated [47]. Therefore, as the amount of BP&NCDs added increased, the T5 % of EP/BP&NCDs composites showed a decreasing trend. Additionally, according to Table S1, the addition of BP&NCDs can improve the char residue rate of the epoxy resin system at 700 °C. For example, the residual char yield of EP at 700 °C was 17.43 %, even though that of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material was 23.63 % at the same temperature. Furthermore, combining Fig. 6(b) with Table S1, the addition of BP&NCDs could reduce the maximum rate of thermal weight loss (MLR) of the epoxy cure system. The MLR of EP and EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites were 14.66 %/min and 10.67 %/min, respectively. This was attributed to the presence of FPBA and DOPO, which improved the char-forming ability of the epoxy resin system and hindered the progress of polymer reactions, thus reducing the MLR of the reaction, and encouraging the formation of the char layer. According to the thermogravimetric data, it was seen that the initial decomposition temperature (T5 %) of the EP/BP&NCDs composites showed a downward trend, which was mainly due to the degradation of DOPO on BP&NCDs. However, the phosphorus-containing flame retardant could decompose into phosphates, which was beneficial for the formation of a carbon layer and increased the char residue content of the matrix at 700 °C.

Under air atmosphere (Fig. 6(c-d)), the decomposition process became more complicated due to the presence of oxygen. The DTG plots of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites presented two peaks, showing two stages of decomposition. The first stage of decomposition occurred at 280 °C-310 °C, which corresponded to the decomposition of the epoxy resin network chain segments. The second stage occurred at 550 °C-600 °C, corresponding to the further oxidation of the degradation products from the first stage and their undergoing complex chemical reactions with air. In addition, the T5 %, Tmax1, and Tmax2 of the EP/BP&NCDs composites were relatively lower compared to those of EP, while the residual carbon rate was improved. This was mainly because as the content of BP&NCDs increased, BP&NCDs decomposed into carbon earlier in the decomposition process, protecting the epoxy resin matrix. In conclusion, the addition of BP&NCDs to epoxy resin improved the thermal stability of the epoxy resin system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal mechanical properties of EP/BP&NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para:
To investigate the thermal and mechanical properties of the epoxy curing system, DMA and DSC tests were conducted on EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites. Fig. 7 and Table 1 displayed the test results. The crosslinking density (V e) was calculated using classical rubber elasticity theory [48]:
(1)
V e
=
E ′
/ 3 RT
Here, E′ represents the storage modulus taken 30 °C above Tg. R represents the ideal gas constant, and T represents the thermodynamic temperature of Tg + 30 °C. The DMA results in Table 1 showed that the crosslinking density (V e) of pure EP, EP/1 %-BP&NCDs, EP/3 %-BP&NCDs and EP/5 %-BP&NCDs was calculated to be 1765.2, 1852.33, 1904.20 and 2251.86 mol/m³, respectively. Since the amount of the curing agent DDM in the four samples were the same, the increasing of crosslinking density was attributed to the participation of the flame retardant BP&NCDs in the crosslinking during curing process. To further prove whether BP&NCDs participated in the cross-linking reaction of the epoxy resin, DSC testing was utilized to observe whether the exothermic peak appeared. A mixture sample made of 25 wt% of BP&NCDs and epoxy resin was stirred and then was heated during DSC testing. The DSC results showed that a distinct exothermic peak was observed in Fig S2, which meant that a ring-opening reaction of epoxy molecular occurred as the existence of BP&NCDs. Therefore, it could be concluded that the as-synthesized BP&NCDs was a kind of reactive flame retardant and could generate chemical crosslinking. Besides, due to the nanosize of carbon dots, it could play a role of physical crosslinking points. Overall, it was concluded that the increasing in the crosslink density of EP/BP&NCDs came from both the chemical crosslinking and physical crosslinking. Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites were recorded for comparison. It could be observed from Fig. S3 that the absorption peak of epoxy group at 910 cm⁻¹ gradually decreased as BP&NCDs was added into epoxy resin, meanwhile, a new absorption peak of B-O at 1340 cm⁻¹ appeared, which meant that the BP&NCDs reacted with epoxy resin and brought in boronate ester group B-O bond in EP/BP&NCDs composites.

DMA result showed that the storage modulus of EP/BP&NCDs composites showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing, and it was consistently higher than the storage modulus of pure EP at 1296.3 MPa. Specifically, the storage modulus of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material was 1441.3 MPa. This indicates that the addition of BP&NCDs can enhance the rigidity of the epoxy matrix. The glass transition temperature (Tg) serves as an important parameter for evaluating the thermal performance of epoxy resin, affecting its properties and applications. The Tg of pure EP was 167.3 °C, as shown in Fig. 7(b, c) and Table 1. With the addition of BP&NCDs, the Tg of the epoxy cure system initially decreased, then increased, and finally decreased again. On one hand, adding DOPO and FPBA as functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots provides additional reaction sites, promoting the formation of more bonds between molecules, which increases the crosslinking density and raises the Tg of the EP system. On the other hand, with the addition of BP&NCDs, the free volume of the EP system increases, providing the necessary space for molecular segment movement, which facilitates easier mobility of the segments and consequently lowers the Tg of the epoxy resin system. As for EP/1 %-BP&NCDs composite, the decrease in Tg was mainly due to the dominance of volume effects. As for the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite, the interaction between crosslinking density and volume effects influenced each other, resulting in a Tg of 167.6 °C, which was close to the Tg value of pure EP. However, as for EP/5 %-BP&NCDs composite, the drastic decrease in Tg despite a significant increase in crosslinking density was attributed to the excessive addition of BP&NCDs, which led to over-crosslinking and disrupted the orderliness of the epoxy resin molecular chains, resulting in a decrease in Tg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties and static water contact angle of EP/BP&NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: In three-point bending and impact tests, the mechanical characteristics of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites were assessed, with the results depicted in Fig. 8 . Compared to EP, EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited superior toughness. As shown in Fig. 8(a), during the three-point bending experiment, EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited a more pronounced bending trend when subjected to ultimate stress compared to EP. The stress-strain curves and histograms of bending and impact performance were shown in Fig. 8(b, c). The bending strength, bending modulus, and impact strength of EP was 66.97 MPa, 2037.49 MPa, and 5.15 kJ/m², respectively. Additionally, when EP/BP&NCDs were added, the bending strength, bending modulus, and impact strength of EP were all increased. The bending strength, bending modulus, and impact strength of EP/BP&NCDs exhibited a trend of first increasing and then decreasing with the increase in their addition. This could be attributed to excessive addition causing uneven dispersion and aggregation of BP&NCDs in EP, thereby leading to a decrease in mechanical properties. With respect to bending strength, bending modulus, and impact strength, the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material demonstrated exceptional mechanical properties, measuring 123.47 MPa, 2695.06 MPa, and 6.71 kJ/m², respectively. This was mainly due to BP&NCDs acting as nano-fillers uniformly distributed in EP, forming a good interfacial interaction. When subjected to external forces, stress could be effectively transferred to the EP matrix through BP&NCDs, which might also prevent the expansion of microcracks, thereby imparting excellent mechanical properties.

The fracture surface SEM images of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites quenched in liquid nitrogen visually demonstrate the enhanced toughness of the EP/BP&NCDs composites, as shown in Fig. 8(e). The fracture surface of EP shows signs of brittle fracture, which is common in thermosetting materials, and has a smooth and even surface. The addition of BP&NCDs made the fracture surface rougher, exhibiting features of toughness fracture such as folds and wrinkles. Through analysis of the SEM images of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites etched in anhydrous ethanol for 14 days, it was evident that BP&NCDs were uniformly distributed in the EP matrix in the composites with 1 % and 3 % addition levels, while obvious aggregation occurred in the composites with 5 % addition level. Additionally, contact angle tests were conducted on EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites, with the results shown in Fig. 8(g) Compared to EP, the static water contact angle of EP/BP&NCDs composites was greater than 90°, indicating good hydrophobicity of EP/BP&NCDs composites, which might be related to the favorable interface compatibility brought about by the incorporation of BP&NCDs.

In the modern electronic packaging industry, selecting the appropriate epoxy resin was crucial. Epoxy resins with excellent dielectric properties ensured that electronic devices operated stably and reliably under various conditions, extending their lifespan and enhancing overall performance. In electronic components, a high dielectric constant in high-frequency circuits could lead to greater parasitic capacitance, resulting in signal delay and distortion. High dielectric loss could cause increased energy loss and heat generation. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the dielectric constant decreased when varying proportions of BP&NCDs were added to the epoxy resin, indicating that the EP/BP&NCDs composites had low polarity. The dielectric constant of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs was the lowest, at approximately 3.5. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 9(b), the EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited lower dielectric loss compared to EP. This was likely due to the good dispersion of BP&NCDs within the EP, forming a stable structure with the matrix. Additionally, the low polarity of 4-formylphenylboronic acid, used to modify the surface of NCDs, reduced the overall polarization of the material. [49]

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant performance of EP/BP&NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: UL-94 and LOI tests were applied to test the flame retardancy of EP and EP/BP&NCDs. Table S2 and Fig. 10 displayed the test results. In accordance with Fig. 10(a), pure EP, as a combustible material, underwent intense burning during the UL-94 test, producing a large amount of smoke and accompanied by a dripping phenomenon. The dripping droplets ignited the bottom cotton ball, failing to meet the UL-94 classification standards. After adding BP&NCDs, self-extinguishing occurred during the combustion of the epoxy cure system. Specifically, the UL-94 classification of EP/1 %-BP&NCDs composite material reached the V-1 level, while the UL-94 classification of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs and EP/5 %-BP&NCDs composites reached the V-0 level. The Limiting Oxygen Index (LOI) is an important parameter for characterizing the combustion behavior of materials. When the LOI value exceeds 27 %, it indicates that the material has flame-retardant properties. According to Fig. 10(b), the addition of BP&NCDs increased the LOI value of the epoxy curing system. The LOI values of the composites made from EP, EP/1 %-BP&NCDs, EP/3 %-BP&NCDs, and EP/5 %-BP&NCDs were 25.1 %, 29.5 %, 31.3 %, and 32.9 %, respectively. These tests indicate that adding a certain amount of BP&NCDs to the epoxy resin matrix can effectively enhance the flame-retardant properties of the epoxy resin. This was explained by the phosphide and borate produced during the combustion of BP&NCDs, which formed a protective layer on the resin surface and demonstrated superior flame retardant properties.

As an ideal instrument for characterizing the combustion performance of materials, cone calorimeter tests were conducted to simulate the real burning environment of EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites in fires. The test results mainly included TTI, PHRR, HRR, THR, and TSP, as shown in Fig. 10 and Table 2 . Ignition time (TTI), as a crucial criterion for material fire resistance evaluation, indicated the threshold of material in actual combustion. With the addition of BP&NCDs, TTI decreased, mainly due to the introduced DOPO phenyl ring structure having lower thermal stability. In Fig. 10(c), the most important performance parameters for determining fire intensity were the heat release rate (HRR) and peak heat release rate (PHRR). Additionally, it was possible to represent the fire growth index (FGI) as the ratio of the time to peak heat release rate (TTPHRR) to the PHRR. The fire hazard that resulted increases with FGI [50]. The PHRR and FGI of EP were 1078 kW/m2 and 9.14 kW/(m2·s), respectively. When the addition of BP&NCDs exceeded 3 %, both PHRR and FGI significantly decreased. The PHRR and FGI of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs and EP/5 %-BP&NCDs composites were 701 kW/m2, 7.46 kW/(m2·s), 644 kW/m2 and 4.10 kW/(m2·s), respectively, indicating excellent fire safety. In addition, combining Total Heat Release (THR) with Heat Release Rate (HRR) enables a more precise assessment of the material's flame-retardant properties. The addition of BP&NCDs effectively reduced the THR during the combustion of the epoxy cure system. The THR of EP/3 %-BP&NCDs was 69 MJ/m2, much less than the 94 MJ/m2 of pure EP, as shown in Fig. 10(d). This might be because during combustion, BP&NCDs in EP/BP&NCDs composites catalyzed the creation of a protective layer of char. Moreover, the cone calorimeter test was capable of measuring smoke generation during combustion. Total smoke production (TSP) represented the cumulative smoke generated per unit sample area during combustion, exactly as illustrated in Fig. 10(e) and Table 2. Based on the cone calorimeter (CCT) test results, it was seen that the TSP of EP/1 %BP&NCDs and EP/3 %BP&NCDs was lower than that of pure EP, but EP/5 %BP&NCDs had a higher TSP than pure EP. Fig. 10(e) showed that compared with pure EP, EP/5 %-BP&NCDs started to produce smoke at an earlier time, and kept a longer combustion process, causing the TSP higher than the other samples. This could be explained by the reason that BP&NCDs mainly exerted a gas-phase flame-retardant mechanism. As shown in Fig. 11(b)-(c), as the addition of BP&NCDs gradually increased, the compactness of the formed char layer became improved at first and then became worse again. The char layer of EP/5 %-BP&NCDs was relatively less dense. Thus, more incompletely-burned polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons were likely to be released during combustion. Moreover, non-flammable gases like nitrogen (N₂) and ammonia (NH₃) from the decomposition of EP/5 %-BP&NCDs also increased gradually. This made the gas flow break through the char-layer barrier from within and then released more smoke particles, leading to a significant increase in both smoke concentration and total smoke production. It could be seen in Table 2 that during the combustion of the epoxy curing system, the addition of BP&NCDs can reduce the generation of carbon dioxide. In conclusion, according to the above analysis, adding BP&NCDs to EP had a positive impact on thermal decomposition and char formation during the combustion process, thus improving the fire safety of EP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The characterization of residual carbon in EP/BP&NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Digital and SEM images of the cone calorimetry residue of EP/BP&NCDs composites were obtained in order to observe the macroscopic and microscopic morphology of the residual carbon. As demonstrated in Fig. 11(a), the residual carbon of pure EP became loose, irregular, and decreased significantly in volume after cone calorimetry treatment, while the residual carbon of EP/BP&NCDs composites became denser, fluffier, more continuous, and with a significant increase in carbon yield after cone calorimetry treatment. As shown in Fig. 11(b) and (c), the outer and inner layers of the residual char were examined under a scanning electron microscope to further assess the condition of the residual char. The outer layer of residual carbon after cone calorimetry treatment of pure EP was loose, while the inner layer was smooth. In contrast, the residual carbon layer after cone calorimetry treatment of EP/BP&NCDs composites was denser and more continuous, with a large number of pores and expanded bubble-like structures in the inner layer. The reason for This phenomenon may be that DOPO introduced in BP&NCDs generates phosphoric acid when thermally decomposed [51], covering the surface of EP to create a dense and continuous carbon layer. Throughout the thermal degradation process, this continuous and dense layer of carbon could stop heat from being transferred, flammable fragments and volatile small molecules from escaping, which caused the inner layer to become more porous and expanded into bubble-like structures.

To further investigate the cleanliness and graphitization degree of the residual carbon in EP and EP/BP&NCDs composites, Raman spectroscopy characterization was conducted, as shown in Fig. 11(d). The characteristic peaks at 1344 cm−1 and 1587 cm−1, respectively, correspond to the D band (characteristic peak of amorphous carbon) and G band (peak of CC bond stretching vibration in ordered carbon). The integrated area ratio of the D band to the G band, or ID/IG, was commonly used to represent the graphitization degree of residual carbon. A smaller R value denotes a higher graphitization degree, which was favorable to the formation of dense and ordered carbon layers, thereby preventing heat transfer and oxygen isolation. For pure EP, the D value was 2.26. Compared to this, the D values of residual carbon in EP/BP&NCDs composites were all reduced. The D value of residual carbon in EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material was 1.49, indicating that the addition of BP&NCDs promotes the generation of continuous and dense carbon layers, consistent with the SEM observation results. The addition of BP&NCDs in the epoxy system results in denser and more continuous carbon layers after combustion, protecting the epoxy matrix from further exposure to oxygen and heat transfer, thereby achieving flame retardancy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flame retardant mechanism of EP/BP&NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the gas products produced during combustion, TG-FTIR tests were carried out on EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composites, and Fig. 12 (a, b) displayed the findings. The thermal decomposition products of the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite material exhibit characteristic peaks consistent with the conventional decomposition products of the epoxy matrix. Specifically, the peak at 3658 cm−1 indicated water or phenolic derivatives; aliphatic components were indicated by peaks at 2976 and 1178 cm−1; CO2 was represented by 2360 cm−1; compounded with aromatic rings were suggested by peaks at 1514 and 1619 cm−1[52,53]; and ether compounds were indicated by peaks at 1262 cm−1 [54]. Additionally, the thermal decomposition products of the EP/3 %-BP&NCDs composite exhibited the emergence of new characteristic peaks, with the peak at 1120 cm−1 attributed to the stretching vibration of CN (alkyl carbon), the peak at 1340 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of B-O, and the peaks at 752 cm−1 and 832 cm−1 corresponded to the PO-, P(O)-OH groups. This was primarily due to the thermal decomposition of the low-thermal-stability naphthalene ring structure P-O-C bond in DOPO and the B-OH bond that was not involved in the curing of the epoxy resin at 330 °C, consistent with the TGA testing analysis results.

According to the characterization analysis above, the epoxy composite material made with BP&NCDs as fillers exhibited excellent flame retardant performance, as shown in Fig. 12(c). When the EP/BP&NCDs composite material burned, from the perspective of flame retardancy in the condensed phase, first, the DOPO introduced in BP&NCDs generated phosphoric acid when heated [55,56], covering the surface of EP to form a flame-retardant layer that separated the heat transfer and oxygen contact. Secondly, during the decomposition of FPBA, boric acid was generated. At elevated temperatures, phosphoric acid and boric acid could undergo a reaction to produce a highly thermally stable compound, BPO4. This compound played a crucial role in fire retardancy by forming a protective layer during combustion. This layer effectively covered the surface of the resin, thereby impeding direct contact between oxygen and combustion products with the resin, consequently retarding the combustion process. [57] Finally, because of their numerous microporous structure and large specific surface area, NCDs exhibited certain adsorption abilities, which could capture or consume some active free radicals during combustion, further enhancing the carbon layer [58]. A continuous and dense carbon layer helps in heat transfer and oxygen isolation, thereby enhancing the flame-retardant properties of the material. During the combustion process in the gas phase flame retardant part, DOPO breaks down to create free radicals PO· and HPO·. These radicals could then consume the H· and O· produced during combustion, break the chain reaction of combustion and produce the flame retardant effect [59]. In summary, when EP/BP&NCDs composites were burned, a continuous and dense carbon layer was formed thanks to the synergistic action of phosphorus and boron in CDs.

In this work, NCDs derived from DDM as the raw material were successfully modified to obtain BP&NCDs, which were then incorporated into EP to produce EP/BP&NCDs composites. EP/BP&NCDs composites demonstrated exceptional transparency, photoluminescence capability, and mechanical properties. Due to the introduction of FPBA in BP&NCDs, the dielectric properties of EP/BP&NCDs were improved, resulting in reduced dielectric constant and dielectric loss. Besides, the introduction of DOPO and FPBA led to a significant enhancement in the flame-retardant performance of the EP/BP&NCDs composites. With only a 3 % addition, the EP/BP&NCDs composite material achieved an LOI value of 31.3 % and obtained a UL-94 V-0 rating. Moreover, in comparison to pure EP, the EP/BP&NCDs composites exhibited reductions of 34.9 %, 26.6 %, and 15.6 % in PHRR, THR, and TSP, respectively. This work would provide a reasonable approach for the preparation of multifunctional flame-retardant epoxy resins, thus opening up avenues for their potential applications in modern electronics industry.

Lingzhi Wang: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hua Liu: Software, Investigation, Data curation. Birong Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Kaibin He: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yiting Xu: Supervision. Conghui Yuan: Supervision. Lizong Dai: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52173081).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.polymdegradstab.2025.111275.
